title: McLintock!

evidence.note: Batjac (the Wayne family company) failed to renew the film's copyright in 1991, a lapse that was litigated around home-video rights in the 1990s and is unusually well documented for a color star vehicle. Primary renewal-search scan not yet attached — held at 'likely'.
Frank De Vol score plus the song 'Love in the Country'; separate registration status not verified here.
Original screenplay by James Edward Grant (loosely riffing on The Taming of the Shrew, which is Shakespeare and free).
Paramount's restored HD releases carry new protectable elements; archival prints are the free source.

editorial:
A John Wayne–Maureen O'Hara comedy in color from a major star's own company — and it still fell out of copyright, because in 1991 somebody at Batjac missed the renewal deadline. McLintock! became the highest-profile proof that renewal failure wasn't just a B-movie problem, and the family spent years fighting cut-rate VHS releases they couldn't stop.

For creators: the print circulates freely, the screenplay is original, and the risks are the era's usual pair — an unverified music chain and protected modern restorations.
faq: How did a John Wayne movie end up public domain?
The same way as everyone else's: the 28-year renewal deadline (1991 for this film) was missed. Star power didn't file paperwork.
Yes, from archival prints; prefer replacing the audio where the score is prominent, since the music chain is unverified.
